claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you enquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm enquiring about my son Alex, who's 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'd like to ask about Alex's current situation.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he still in school, college, or an approved training program?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, he's just left school actually. He's signed up with the careers service to figure out what he wants to do next, but he hasn't started anything yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful information. When Alex left school, did he leave approved education or training, and approximately how long ago did this happen? 

I'm asking because there may be a 20-week extension period available for 16 or 17 year olds who leave education or training and register with a government-sponsored careers service.

**Can you tell me roughly when Alex left school and registered with the careers service?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he left school quite recently. He registered with the careers service straight after leaving, so he's within that period you mentioned.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great - it sounds like Alex may be covered under the 20-week extension period.

Now I need to ask a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a few more questions:

**Does Alex live with you, or are you contributing financially to his upkeep (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Has Alex started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or is he claiming any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't started any job like that - he's not working 24 hours a week or anything. And he's not claiming any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also ask:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

Even though Alex has left school, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for up to **20 weeks** because he's 16 years old and has registered with a government-sponsored careers service. This extension period applies specifically to 16 or 17 year olds who leave education or training and register with the careers service or armed services.

Since you:
- Live in the UK
- Alex lives with you at home
- Alex isn't working 24 hours or more per week
- Alex isn't claiming benefits in his own right
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him

You meet all the eligibility requirements during this 20-week period. If Alex starts approved education, training, or an apprenticeship within this time, your Child Benefit can continue beyond the 20 weeks (up until he turns 20, as long as he remains in approved education or training).

If you have any other questions about Child Benefit, please feel free to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has recently left school. He registered with a government-sponsored careers service straight after leaving education. Under the Child Benefit rules, you can continue to claim Child Benefit for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Alex meets this criteria as he is 16, has left school, and registered with the careers service within the relevant timeframe. Additionally, the claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), Alex lives with them (so they are responsible for him), Alex is not working 24 hours or more per week, Alex is not claiming benefits in his own right, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. All eligibility requirements are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 16-year-old son Alex. Alex has recently left school and registered with the careers service, which qualifies for the 20-week extension period available for 16-17 year olds who leave education and register with a government-sponsored careers service. The claimant lives in the UK, Alex lives with them, and no one else is claiming for Alex.

All eligibility requirements are met.
